summary: The post argues that brilliant software engineers are wasting their talents on morally questionable or trivial work at financial institutions and defense contractors, instead of solving the world's real problems. Drawing on Zimbardo's prison experiment and Milgram's obedience studies, Khailo suggests that smart people aren't inherently evil but are shaped by their contexts into rationalizing harmful or useless work.
key_points: Talented programmers are drawn to lucrative but morally dubious industries like finance and defense
Software has become ubiquitous and touches every aspect of modern life, giving it outsized societal influence
People are shaped by their context, as demonstrated by the Stanford prison experiment and Milgram's obedience studies
The tech industry needs to redirect its talent toward solving meaningful problems
money_quotes: As flies swarm to shit, we have these MIT grads buzzing around financial institutions.
We have these blokes deciding for themselves that being rich is cool, and it's best to write software that skims the cream off the top of some morally reprehensible if not murderous dealings.
Because we make software in context. We are a shape that is molded by the world. It is just that some people forget what shape they became.

The world has so many problems to solve, how can we get people solving them. I mean, sure the MIT genius working for a financial company is solving problems. I guess, Mr Software, constipation is a problem too.
key_insight: The software industry's brightest minds aren't evil, but they've been contextually shaped into rationalizing work that enriches themselves while ignoring the world's real problems.
